title:
Warhammer Guild
warhammerguild.com › lore › all-warhammer-40k-factions
All Warhammer 40k Factions —
content:
Warhammer 40,000 features over 20 distinct playable factions, each with its own unique playstyle, aesthetic, and lore. These factions are broadly organized into three grand alliances — the Imperium of Man, the forces of Chaos, and the various Xenos (alien) races — each competing for dominance in a galaxy consumed by endless war. This guide provides a complete list and overview of every playable faction in Warhammer 40k, along with playstyle summaries, difficulty ratings, and links to detailed army guides.
Whether you are a new player choosing your first army or a veteran looking for a comprehensive faction reference, this guide covers everything you need to know about the factions of Warhammer 40,000.
How Many Factions Are in Warhammer 40k?
Warhammer 40,000 has approximately 21 to 24 playable factions, depending on how you count subfactions and supplemental armies. The core playable factions in the current edition include: Space Marines, Astra Militarum, Adeptus Custodes, Adepta Sororitas, Adeptus Mechanicus, Imperial Knights, Imperial Agents, Chaos Space Marines, Death Guard, Thousand Sons, World Eaters, Chaos Daemons, Chaos Knights, Aeldari, Drukhari, Tyranids, Orks, Necrons, T’au Empire, Genestealer Cults, and Leagues of Votann. Space Marines can further be divided into numerous Chapters (such as Ultramarines, Blood Angels, Dark Angels, Space Wolves, and Black Templars), each with unique rules and playstyles.
The factions of Warhammer 40k are divided into three broad categories: Imperium (humanity’s galaxy-spanning civilization), Chaos (the corrupted forces of the Warp), and Xenos (alien races). Each faction offers a completely different gameplay experience, from the elite few-model armies of the Adeptus Custodes to the overwhelming hordes of the Tyranids.
TheImperium of Manis the largest human civilization in the galaxy, spanning millions of worlds and ruled (in theory) by the Emperor of Mankind from the Golden Throne on Terra. Imperium factions represent the various military arms of this vast empire, from the superhuman Space Marines to the countless regiments of the Astra Militarum.
Space Marinesare the iconic faction of Warhammer 40,000 — genetically-enhanced superhuman soldiers organized into Chapters of approximately 1,000 warriors each. They are the Imperium’s greatest warriors, each one stronger, faster, and tougher than any baseline human. Space Marines are the most popular faction in the game and offer a versatile, well-rounded playstyle suitable for beginners and veterans alike.
Playstyle:Versatile and well-rounded. Space Marines can be built for shooting, melee, or a balanced approach. Each Chapter provides a different tactical emphasis. They have good durability, solid firepower, and strong melee options.
Difficulty:Beginner — Space Marines are the most beginner-friendly faction with forgiving stats and a wide range of options.
Ultramarines— Tactical flexibility, the “default” Space Marine Chapter
Blood Angels— Aggressive melee specialists with a vampiric theme
Dark Angels— Combined arms with elite Deathwing Terminators and Ravenwing bikers
Space Wolves— Fierce, Viking-themed melee fighters with unique unit options
Black Templars— Zealous crusaders focused on close combat
Imperial Fists— Siege specialists and experts in fortification
Iron Hands— Technology-focused with enhanced vehicle and dreadnought support
Salamanders— Flame and melta weapon specialists with strong durability
Raven Guard— Stealth and infiltration specialists
White Scars— Fast-attack specialists focusing on speed and mobility
Deathwatch— Xenos-hunting specialists with mixed-unit kill teams
Astra Militarum (Imperial Guard)
Astra Militarum, formerly known as the Imperial Guard, represents the vast conventional military forces of the Imperium — billions of ordinary human soldiers who hold the line against the horrors of the 41st Millennium through sheer numbers, heavy firepower, and armored might. Where Space Marines are elite surgical strike forces, the Astra Militarum is the sledgehammer of the Imperium.
Playstyle:Horde-style infantry combined with powerful tanks and artillery. Astra Militarum excels at ranged firepower and bringing overwhelming numbers to bear. Individual soldiers are weak, but they are cheap and numerous.
Difficulty:Intermediate — Requires careful positioning and order management; individual models are fragile.
Adeptus Custodesare the Emperor’s personal bodyguard — the greatest warriors in the Imperium, each one individually superior to even a Space Marine. Created by the Emperor Himself using a more refined and intensive process than the creation of Astartes, each Custodian is a one-man army clad in auramite armor and wielding master-crafted weapons.
Playstyle:Elite, low model count army. Every Custodian is extremely powerful and durable. Games are played with very few models (sometimes under 20), making each casualty significant. They excel in melee combat but have solid shooting options.
Difficulty:Intermediate — Easy to learn due to low model count, but every tactical decision matters because you have so few models.
Adepta Sororitas (Sisters of Battle)
Adepta Sororitas, the Sisters of Battle, are the military arm of the Ecclesiarchy — the Imperium’s state church. They are fanatically devoted warriors who wield bolters and flamers while invoking the Emperor’s miracles on the battlefield. Their faith is so strong that it manifests as literal divine power, granting them miraculous abilities.
Playstyle:Mid-range shooting with strong melee options. Sisters of Battle combine bolter-heavy firepower with faith-based mechanics (Miracle Dice) that allow them to guarantee key rolls. They have strong synergy between units and reward aggressive play.
Difficulty:Intermediate — The Miracle Dice system adds a layer of resource management that requires practice.
Adeptus Mechanicusare the tech-priests and machine-warriors of Mars, the Imperium’s technological priesthood. They worship the Machine God (the Omnissiah) and maintain the Imperium’s technology — everything from starships to bolters. On the battlefield, they field augmented cyborg soldiers, arcane weapons, and walking war-machines.
Playstyle:Ranged-focused army with strong shooting at multiple ranges. Adeptus Mechanicus units tend to be fragile but deadly, with excellent special weapons and a mix of infantry, cavalry, and vehicles. They reward careful positioning and target priority.
Difficulty:Advanced — Complex rules interactions, many special abilities, and relatively fragile units that punish positioning errors.
Imperial Knightsare towering war machines piloted by noble houses — each Knight is a walking fortress standing several stories tall, armed with weapons capable of leveling buildings. An Imperial Knights army consists of very few models (sometimes as few as 3-5), each one a massive, powerful centerpiece.
Playstyle:Ultra-elite, ultra-low model count. Every model is a devastating war machine. Knights excel at destroying vehicles and monsters with their massive weapons. Games hinge on keeping your few models alive and choosing targets wisely.
Difficulty:Beginner to Intermediate — Very few models to learn, but limited tactical flexibility and vulnerability to certain matchups.
Imperial Agentsrepresent the shadowy organizations that operate across the Imperium — the Inquisition, assassins, the Sisters of Silence, and other specialist operatives. They can be fielded as their own army or attached to other Imperial forces to provide unique capabilities.
Playstyle:Highly specialized units that fill specific roles. Imperial Agents are often used to supplement other armies with assassins, inquisitors, and other unique characters. As a standalone force, they offer a diverse but eclectic mix of elite operatives.
Difficulty:Advanced — Requires deep knowledge of the game to use effectively; relies on synergy and specific counter-play.
The forces ofChaosrepresent the corrupted enemies of the Imperium — war
summary: Warhammer 40,000 features over 20 distinct playable factions, each with its own unique playstyle, aesthetic, and lore. These factions are broadly organized into three grand alliances — the Imperium of Man, the forces of Chaos, and the various Xenos (alien) races — each competing for dominance in a galaxy consumed by endless war. Difficulty:Intermediate — Requires careful positioning and order management; individual models are fragile. Difficulty:Intermediate — Easy to learn due to low model count, but every tactical decision matters because you have so few models. Difficulty:Intermediate — The Miracle Dice system adds a layer of resource management that requires practice. Difficulty:Advanced — Requires deep knowledge of the game to use effectively; relies on synergy and specific counter-play.

Union of Progressive Peoples
SAVE
EDIT

"Is that damage?"
"It looks like damage."
This article, or a section of this article, does not meet Xenopedia's standards. Please help by improving the page.

Union of Progressive Peoples
Headquarters:
Earth
Founded by:
China
Russia
Germany
Spain
Years active:
From 2108 onward
Territory:
Spinward Outer Veil
Rimward Outer Veil
Spinward Outer Rim Territories
Ethnicity:
Human
Allies:
Hyperdyne Corporation
Rivals:
Independent Core System Colonies
Three World Empire
United Americas
"Trust your enemy to be your enemy, but never trust your neighbor to be your friend."
―UPP saying (from Alien: The Roleplaying Game)

The Union of Progressive Peoples, often referred to as the UPP, was a socialist interstellar superpower formed in 2108 by China and Russia in response to the political might of the Three World Empire and the United Americas. The Union of Progressive Peoples was the only major power of its era not to be influenced by corporate concerns, labeling other states as "corporatists", and maintained the UPP Armed Collective, which represented the largest fighting force in the Middle Heavens. Despite its formidable military might, the UPP's widespread territory was lacking in resources, with its colonies forced to ration foodstuffs such as bread. In 2186, the UPP attempted to commandeer the United Nations Interstellar Settlement Corps' Great Mother Mission to the long–lost Far Spinward Colonies and take control of the region, though the coup was ultimately thwarted.

Contents
1 Overview
2 History
2.1 Formation and early activities
2.2 The Darkstar Program
3 Organization
3.1 Leadership
3.2 Space Operating Forces
3.3 Ministry of Space Security
4 Locations
5 Diplomacy and Relationships
5.1 Three World Empire
5.2 United Americas
5.3 Weyland-Yutani
5.4 Lasalle Bionational
5.5 Seegson
6 Non-canon appearances
6.1 Alien 3: The Unproduced Screenplay
6.2 Aliens: Infestation
7 Appearances
8 References
Overview

The UPP was a powerful socialist bloc that controlled a vast albeit resource-poor sector of space, with a minimal presence in the Core Systems and major presences in the Outer Veil, the Outer Rim Territories, and the Frontier. Virtually since its formation, the UPP was stuck in a state of cold war with the United Americas, and although the UPP lagged behind its rivals in the space and arms race, they still maintained a formidable interstellar fighting force.[1] Indicative of their inferior technological prowess, most of the UPP's advanced technology was apparently reverse-engineered from that of the Weyland-Yutani Corporation[2] and Lasalle Bionational.[3]

History
Formation and early activities

The Union of Progressive Peoples was created in the wake of a civil war on the former Paraguayan—and now United Americas—colony world of Torin Prime in 2108.[4] Former European Union member states Germany and Spain joined with Russia in direct response to the perceived combined threat of the Three World Empire and the United Americas, while additional joiners included Vietnam and several other Asian and Eastern European nations.[5] The UPP received massive support from the Chinese/Asian Nations Cooperative, which initially declined to fold into the UPP due to ideological conflict.[6] Between 2108 and 2111, several worlds from the spinward Outer Veil expressed dissatisfaction with corporate greed and seceded from the 3WE, while others were annexed. Lacking the forces needed to retake those colonies, the 3WE chose not to intervene.[6] In 2117, the UPP became a member of the United Nations Interstellar Settlement Corps, a restructured version of the United Nations Interplanetary Corps.[7] In the first half of the 2150s, the UPP supported rebels on the 3WE world of Helene 215 against the '51 New Deal and left the UNISC after the United Americas annexed the planet and the Marines raided one of their secret supply bases on Lebanon II. During the Core Conflict, the Hyades Cluster mining colonies revolted and seceded to the UPP, leading to clashes with the Marines until the '65 UA/UPP ceasefire.[8]

The UPP attempted to subjugate the Frontier world of 8 Eta Boötis A III in 2162,[4] but an independent planet, supported by the planet's old regime, requested the United Americas' help, which led to the infamous Tientsin Campaign. The three year-long conflict decimated the once lush world, and left it in ruins with no decisive winner.[9] Following that incident, the UPP was locked in a state of cold war with the United States Colonial Marine Corps and United Americas[2] and signed the Pang Ho Treaty.[10] In 2179, the UPP was rumored to be behind the destruction of both the Hadley's Hope colony on LV-426 and a Colonial Marine rescue team by nuking the site from orbit.[2] In the same year, the UPP station Rodina, located on the border of the Neroid Sector, suffered a severe unidentified viral contagion and was destroyed by the UPP battlecruiser SSV Nikolai Stolko.[11]

In the early 2180s, due to overpopulation and poor resources, the UPP intensified its efforts in colonizing the Frontier, launching more incursions into colonial space; as a result, standoffs between its Space Operating Forces and the United States Colonial Marine Corps became increasingly common.[2] Further complications arose; United Americas Intelligence suspected the UPP of supporting the J'har rebel faction on Torin Prime and allying with them to enact an uprising in the Tartarus Sector.[12] In 2183, the UPP discovered a cache of David 7 synthetics at the former Weyland Corp Corinth Colony on the annexed planet of KOI-571.02; those synthetics were refurbished as combat androids at the nearby Ouragan Testing Facility and deployed throughout the Union in defiance of the 2151 New Deal agreement, which forbade the use of synthetics in combat.[10]

The Darkstar Program

"Darkstar" was the codename of a covert bioweapon development program headed by the Ministry of Space Security.[13] After obtaining top-secret Weyland-Yutani data on the Xenomorph species, efforts to obtain a specimen of their own were launched. Though initial scouting mission to LV-426 proved fruitless (Wey-Yu sensor markers in the area eventually picked up on UPP incursions), scientists at Rodina Station had eventually managed to create their own Ovomorph,[14] but the egg was more akin to the Neomorphic Egg sacks found on Planet 4. When coming into close contact with the egg, it will emit a cloud of spores, infecting nearby hosts with a human/Xeno hybrid known as a Bodyburster. However, the forced destruction of Rodina following unknown events resulted in the loss of all data on how the creature was created.[15] Luckily, a UPP salvage team would acquire remnants of Plagiarus praepotens cells.[16]

Lab sites were subsequently established across the UA border and Bordline Region. Although unable to reproduce the same creature as before, scientists managed to reconstruct the damaged chromosomes into a Neomorphic genome, successfully conjuring Neomorph specimens.[17] The UPP have since preferred producing these 'Heomopfs' over the traditional Xenomorph XX121, as their extremely short lifespan proved a viable and controllable bioweapon, albeit with the troubling catch of a Neomorph corpse producing Egg sacks, rendering nearby areas hazardous.[18]

After subsequent experiments, Darkstar had also managed to acquire pure Plagiarus praepotens samples, and an effort to create Xenomorph specimens promptly followed; the UPP had since established three Xenomorph hives on remote moons. However, two of the hive's Queens were removed for the purpose of Project Morana, a covert psychological experiment in whic
